title: Glomerular Hematuria
buzzwords: Blood in urine with high-grade proteinuria
Tea-colored or cola-colored urine
Hypertension and declining renal function
Breaks in the glomerular basement membrane
Postinfectious GN, lupus, IgA nephropathy
Donut-shaped RBCs with surface blebs
Dysmorphic RBCs and acanthocytes on microscopy
RBC casts pathognomonic for glomerular source

title: Nonglomerular (Extraglomerular) Hematuria
buzzwords: Bleeding anywhere downstream from the glomerulus
Stones, trauma, infection, or urologic malignancy
Painless gross hematuria in a smoker
Whole clots passed in the urine
Normally shaped RBCs on microscopy
No proteinuria and no RBC casts
Transitional cell bladder cancer on cystoscopy
Cyclophosphamide-induced hemorrhagic cystitis

title: Gross vs Microscopic Hematuria
buzzwords: Incidental finding on routine urinalysis screening
Patient sees bright red or pink urine
Trauma, stones, and hemorrhagic cystitis classically
Naked-eye visible blood versus microscope-only
Whole clots favor lower urinary tract
Asymptomatic dipstick blood without visible change
More than 3 RBCs per high-power field
Visible hematuria defines gross; microscopy defines micro

title: Pseudohematuria and Pigment Mimics
buzzwords: Red or discolored urine without true RBCs
Heme-positive dipstick but no cells on microscopy
Beeturia after dietary beet ingestion
Rifampin, phenazopyridine, doxorubicin discoloration
Crush injury with brown urine and elevated CK
Hemoglobinuria from intravascular hemolysis
Port-wine urine of acute intermittent porphyria
Myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis mimicking hematuria

title: Hematuria Workup Approach
buzzwords: Confirm positive dipstick with urine microscopy
Pyuria plus bacteria points to UTI
Serum creatinine and eGFR for renal function
Noncontrast CT preferred for stones and trauma
Ultrasound first in pregnancy and young children
Cystoscopy when imaging negative, especially smokers
Kidney biopsy when glomerular disease suspected
Dipstick over 90% sensitive for hematuria
